solution.p: We will factor r^{2}-8r+12 where a=1, b=-8, and c=12.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.
For r^{2}-8r+12, since the product of -2 and -6 is 12, and the sum of -2 and -6 is -8, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}-8r+12\amp =\left(r-2\right)\mathopen{}\left(r-6\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (12) = 12.

The number 12 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(12)\amp -1(-12)\amp 2(6)\amp -2(-6)
ewline3(4)\amp -3(-4)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of -8, and this sum happens with (-6)+(-2)=-8 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]r^{2}-8r+12\amp =r^{2}-6r-2r+12
ewline \amp =r\mathopen{}\left(r-6ight)-2\mathopen{}\left(r-6ight)
ewline \amp =\left(r-2ight)\mathopen{}\left(r-6ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in r^{2}-8r+12 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 12, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(12)\amp -1(-12)\amp 2(6)\amp -2(-6)\newline3(4)\amp -3(-4)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with r^{2}-8r+12 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}-8r+12\amp =\left(r-2\right)\mathopen{}\left(r-6\right)\end{aligned}
